message.content:
{
  "materials": {
    "ppt": {
      "templateId": "kamalaniketan-session-12",
      "templateName": "Kamalaniketan Session PPT Template",
      "themeId": "kamalaniketan-modern",
      "title": "The Water Cycle: Grade 5 Science",
      "presentationTitle": "The Water Cycle - Lesson 1 of 3",
      "presentationGoal": "To help students understand the continuous movement of water on Earth through evaporation, condensation, and precipitation.",
      "audience": "Grade 5 Students (Age 10-11)",
      "theme": "Modern Educational Blue",
      "themeTokens": {
        "fonts": {
          "heading": "Outfit",
          "body": "Inter"
        },
        "colors": {
          "primary": "#36ADAA",
          "secondary": "#1EABDA",
          "accent": "#DE8431",
          "background": "#EEF4F7",
          "surface": "#FFFFFF",
          "text": "#0F172A",
          "mutedText": "#64748B"
        },
        "visualStyle": {
          "topBarStyle": "bold color band",
          "cardStyle": "rounded modern educator cards",
          "visualFrameStyle": "large clean visual panel"
        }
      },
      "assetSearchPlan": {
        "preferredSources": [
          "Openverse",
          "Wikimedia Commons"
        ],
        "safeSearch": true,
        "licensingNotes": [
          "Use reusable or public domain assets only."
        ],
        "fallbackStrategy": "Prefer SVG diagrams when external imagery is not needed."
      },
      "licenseChecklist": [
        "Verify reusable license before final export."
      ],
      "presentationWarnings": [
        "Do not include untaught future-session content like the water table or groundwater specifically yet, focus on surface cycle only."
      ],
      "coverageSummary": {
        "learningOutcomesCovered": [
          "Define evaporation",
          "Explain condensation",
          "Describe precipitation"
        ],
        "topicsCovered": [
          "Water Cycle Stages",
          "State Changes of Water"
        ],
        "taughtConceptsCovered": [
          "Sun's role in heating water",
          "Cloud formation process",
          "Rain and snow as precipitation"
        ],
        "omittedContent": []
      },
      "slides": [
        {
          "templateId": "kamalaniketan-session-12",
          "templateSlideKey": "title_identity",
          "templateSlideTitle": "The Water Cycle: Grade 5 Science",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 1,
          "slideType": "hook | concept | comparison | process | summary",
          "slideTitle": "The Amazing Journey of Water",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-Intro"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Introduction to the Water Cycle"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Set the stage and introduce the topic clearly.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Students know today's lesson is about how water moves around Earth.",
          "layout": "Title + Hero Visual",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Subject: Science",
            "Grade 5 - Session 1 of 3",
            "Topic: The Water Cycle"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "The Amazing Journey of Water",
            "Science | Grade 5",
            "Session 1/3: Evaporation, Condensation & Precipitation"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Welcome the class and introduce today's topic.",
            "Explain that water is always moving in a circle around our planet.",
            "Ask students if they have ever wondered where rain comes from or where puddles go after it stops raining."
          ],
          "visualPlan": "Large, high-quality illustration of the Earth with arrows showing water movement (ocean to sky and back).",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual hook for the topic.",
              "searchQuery": "water cycle earth diagram educational",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "Public Domain",
              "attributionText": "NASA / Wikimedia Commons",
              "altText": "Diagram showing the water cycle around Earth.",
              "placementHint": "Full bleed background or large right panel"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": {
            "title": "",
            "type": "",
            "instructions": [],
            "svgCode": ""
          },
          "animationHints": [
            "Fade in title",
            "Animate arrows on the Earth diagram to show movement"
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 2
        },
        {
          "templateId": "kamalaniketan-session-12",
          "templateSlideKey": "learning_outcomes",
          "templateSlideTitle": "What We Will Learn Today",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 2,
          "slideType": "hook | concept | comparison | process | summary",
          "slideTitle": "Today's Learning Goals",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO1",
            "LO2",
            "LO3"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Learning Outcomes"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Clarify expectations and success criteria for the lesson.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Students know exactly what they will be able to do by the end of class.",
          "layout": "List with Icons",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Define evaporation clearly.",
            "Explain how clouds form (condensation).",
            "Describe precipitation types."
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "By the end of this lesson, you will:",
            "1. Define Evaporation",
            "2. Explain Condensation",
            "3. Describe Precipitation"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Read out the three main goals.",
            "Explain that 'Define' means to say what it is, and 'Describe' means to tell us how it happens.",
            "Tell students these are the skills they will practice today."
          ],
          "visualPlan": "Simple icons next to each bullet point: A sun for evaporation, a cloud for condensation, rain drops for precipitation.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual cues for learning outcomes.",
              "searchQuery": "sun icon water drop icon cloud icon flat design",
              "sourceSite": "Openverse",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC BY 4.0",
              "attributionText": "Icon Library",
              "altText": "Icons representing sun, clouds, and rain.",
              "placementHint": "Left of text bullets"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": {
            "title": "",
            "type": "",
            "instructions": [],
            "svgCode": ""
          },
          "animationHints": [
            "Reveal each outcome one by one as you discuss it."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 2
        },
        {
          "templateId": "kamalaniketan-session-12",
          "templateSlideKey": "prerequisite_knowledge",
          "templateSlideTitle": "What We Already Know",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 3,
          "slideType": "hook | concept | comparison | process | summary",
          "slideTitle": "Recall: States of Water",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-Prereq"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "States of Matter"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Activate prior knowledge about solids, liquids, and gases.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Students recall that water can change from one state to another.",
          "layout": "Question & Answer",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Water exists as solid (ice).",
            "Water exists as liquid (water).",
            "Can it become a gas?",
            "Where does the sun fit in?"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Recall Question:",
            "What are the three states of water?",
            "- Solid: Ice",
            "- Liquid: Water",
            "- Gas: ?"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Ask students to name the three states of matter they learned previously.",
            "Prompt them with 'Where does steam come from?' or 'What happens when you boil water?'.",
            "Connect this knowledge: Today we will see how heat turns liquid water into a gas (water vapor)."
          ],
          "visualPlan": "Three simple images side-by-side showing ice, liquid water in a glass, and steam rising.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual reminder of states of matter.",
              "searchQuery": "states of matter ice water steam",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC BY-SA 3.0",
              "attributionText": "Public Domain Science Images",
              "altText": "Images showing ice, liquid water, and steam.",
              "placementHint": "Bottom row of slide"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": {
            "title": "",
            "type": "",
            "instructions": [],
            "svgCode": ""
          },
          "animationHints": [
            "Reveal the question first, wait for answers, then reveal images."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 3
        },
        {
          "templateId": "kamalaniketan-session-12",
          "templateSlideKey": "lesson_hook",
          "templateSlideTitle": "The Mystery of the Drying Puddle",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 4,
          "slideType": "hook | concept | comparison | process | summary",
          "slideTitle": "Where Did It Go?",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-Hook"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Observation & Prediction"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Capture attention with a real-world observation.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Students are curious about the invisible movement of water.",
          "layout": "Scenario Card",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "It rained yesterday.",
            "There was a big puddle outside.",
            "Today, it is sunny and dry!",
            "Where did all that water go?"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "The Mystery of the Drying Puddle",
            "- It rained heavily yesterday.",
            "- A large puddle formed on the road.",
            "- The sun came out today.",
            "- Now, it is completely dry."
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Tell a short story about walking to school and seeing a wet street that dried up by noon.",
            "Ask: 'Did someone mop it? Did the water disappear into thin air?'",
            "Encourage students to guess. This sets up the concept of evaporation."
          ],
          "visualPlan": "Illustration of a sunny day with a dry road where a puddle used to be, perhaps showing faint steam rising.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual context for the hook story.",
              "searchQuery": "sunny street dried puddle",
              "sourceSite": "Openverse",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC BY 4.0",
              "attributionText": "Street Photography",
              "altText": "Sunny day with a dry road.",
              "placementHint": "Full width image"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": {
            "title": "",
            "type": "",
            "instructions": [],
            "svgCode": ""
          },
          "animationHints": [
            "Fade in the puddle first, then fade it out to show it drying."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 3
        },
        {
          "templateId": "kamalaniketan-session-12",
          "templateSlideKey": "topic_introduction",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Introducing the Water Cycle",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 5,
          "slideType": "hook | concept | comparison | process | summary",
          "slideTitle": "The Continuous Journey of Water",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-Intro"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Definition of Water Cycle"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Provide the formal definition and context.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Students understand that water moves in a continuous loop.",
          "layout": "Text + Key Term Highlight",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Water never stops moving.",
            "It changes states constantly.",
            "Key Vocabulary: Evaporation, Condensation, Precipitation."
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "The Water Cycle is the continuous movement of water on Earth.",
            "- It involves changing between liquid, gas, and solid.",
            "- Key Terms:",
            "  - Evaporation",
            "  - Condensation",
            "  - Precipitation"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Define the Water Cycle as a never-ending journey.",
            "Emphasize that water is recycled; it doesn't run out or get created new, just moves around.",
            "Introduce the three key vocabulary words clearly."
          ],
          "visualPlan": "A simple circular diagram showing Earth with arrows looping up and down to represent 'continuous movement'.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual representation of continuity.",
              "searchQuery": "water cycle loop arrow earth",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC BY-SA 3.0",
              "attributionText": "Science Diagrams",
              "altText": "Circular diagram showing water movement.",
              "placementHint": "Center of slide"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": {
            "title": "",
            "type": "",
            "instructions": [],
            "svgCode": ""
          },
          "animationHints": [
            "Animate the arrows to move in a circle."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 3
        },
        {
          "templateId": "kamalaniketan-session-12",
          "templateSlideKey": "core_concept",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Step 1: Evaporation",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 6,
          "slideType": "hook | concept | comparison | process | summary",
          "slideTitle": "Evaporation: Liquid to Gas",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO1"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Evaporation Process"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Teach the first major step of the cycle.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Students understand that heat turns water into invisible gas (vapor).",
          "layout": "Definition + Cause & Effect",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Evaporation is when liquid becomes a gas.",
            "The Sun heats up ocean and lake water.",
            "Water rises as invisible vapor.",
            "Puddles dry because of this."
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Step 1: Evaporation",
            "- Liquid Water + Heat (Sun) = Gas (Vapor)",
            "- Happens in oceans, lakes, and puddles.",
            "- The water rises into the air invisibly."
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Explain that 'Evap' means to disappear or dry up.",
            "Clarify that the water doesn't vanish; it turns into a gas called water vapor which we cannot see.",
            "Reiterate the role of the Sun as the energy source."
          ],
          "visualPlan": "Diagram showing sun rays hitting an ocean, with wavy lines rising up to represent invisible vapor.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visualizing heat and rising vapor.",
              "searchQuery": "sun heating water evaporation diagram",
              "sourceSite": "Openverse",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC BY 4.0",
              "attributionText": "Educational Graphics",
              "altText": "Sun rays hitting water with vapor rising.",
              "placementHint": "Right side"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": {
            "title": "",
            "type": "",
            "instructions": [],
            "svgCode": ""
          },
          "animationHints": [
            "Animate wavy lines rising from the water surface."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 4
        },
        {
          "templateId": "kamalaniketan-session-12",
          "templateSlideKey": "visual_explanation",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Step 2: Condensation",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 7,
          "slideType": "hook | concept | comparison | process | summary",
          "slideTitle": "Condensation: Gas to Liquid",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO2"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Cloud Formation"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Explain how clouds form using a visual process.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Students understand that cooling water vapor creates liquid droplets (clouds).",
          "layout": "Process Flowchart",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Water vapor rises and cools down high in the sky.",
            "Cool gas turns back into tiny liquid drops.",
            "These drops gather to form clouds.",
            "'Conden'sation' means gathering together."
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Step 2: Condensation",
            "- Vapor rises and cools in the sky.",
            "- Gas turns back into tiny liquid droplets.",
            "- Droplets gather to form clouds.",
            "- Analogy: Breath on a cold day."
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Explain that high up, it is colder. The vapor cools down and changes state again.",
            "Use the analogy of breathing out on a cold winter day (seeing your breath) to explain how gas turns to liquid droplets.",
            "Emphasize that clouds are made of millions of tiny water drops."
          ],
          "visualPlan": "Diagram showing vapor rising, cooling, and clumping together into cloud shapes. Include an inset image of a cold glass with condensation on the outside for analogy.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visualizing cloud formation process.",
              "searchQuery": "cloud formation diagram water cycle",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "Public Domain",
              "attributionText": "Science Education Resources",
              "altText": "Diagram of vapor turning into clouds.",
              "placementHint": "Main visual"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": {
            "title": "",
            "type": "",
            "instructions": [],
            "svgCode": ""
          },
          "animationHints": [
            "Animate vapor particles rising and turning into blue droplets that cluster together."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 4
        },
        {
          "templateId": "kamalaniketan-session-12",
          "templateSlideKey": "worked_example",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Real-Life Example: Rain and Snow",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 8,
          "slideType": "hook | concept | comparison | process | summary",
          "slideTitle": "Step 3: Precipitation",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO3"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Precipitation Types"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Model the final step of the cycle with examples.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Students know that precipitation is water falling from clouds in various forms.",
          "layout": "Comparison Table",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Clouds get heavy with water drops.",
            "Gravity pulls them down to Earth.",
            "- Rain: Liquid drops (warm weather).",
            "- Snow: Frozen crystals (cold weather)."
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Step 3: Precipitation",
            "- Clouds become too heavy for water droplets.",
            "- Gravity pulls them down to Earth.",
            "- Forms of precipitation:",
            "  - Rain (Liquid)",
            "  - Snow (Solid/Ice)"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Explain that when clouds get full, they can't hold the water anymore.",
            "Gravity takes over and pulls the water back down.",
            "Discuss how temperature determines if it falls as rain or snow."
          ],
          "visualPlan": "Split image showing a rainy scene (umbrella) and a snowy landscape. Arrows pointing from clouds to ground.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual examples of precipitation types.",
              "searchQuery": "rain vs snow weather",
              "sourceSite": "Openverse",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC BY 4.0",
              "attributionText": "Weather Photography",
              "altText": "Images of rain and snow.",
              "placementHint": "Split screen"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": {
            "title": "",
            "type": "",
            "instructions": [],
            "svgCode": ""
          },
          "animationHints": [
            "Animate rain drops and snow flakes falling from the clouds."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 4
        },
        {
          "templateId": "kamalaniketan-session-12",
          "templateSlideKey": "guided_practice",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Let's Practice: The Cycle in Action",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 9,
          "slideType": "hook | concept | comparison | process | summary",
          "slideTitle": "Activity: Order the Steps",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO1",
            "LO2",
            "LO3"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Sequencing Skills"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Have students actively engage with the sequence.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Students can correctly order the stages of the water cycle.",
          "layout": "Interactive Activity",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "- Evaporation (Sun heats water)",
            "- Condensation (Clouds form)",
            "- Precipitation (Rain/Snow falls)",
            "- Collection (Water gathers)"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Activity: Put the steps in order!",
            "1. Sun heats ocean -> Water vapor rises",
            "2. Vapor cools -> Clouds form",
            "3. Clouds heavy -> Rain/Snow falls",
            "4. Water collects -> Cycle repeats"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Ask students to work in pairs.",
            "Give them the four steps (or show scrambled cards) and ask them to arrange them correctly on their desks or whiteboards.",
            "Walk around to check understanding."
          ],
          "visualPlan": "Four blank boxes with arrows between them, waiting for student input. Or a set of shuffled cards visually represented.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual aid for the sequencing activity.",
              "searchQuery": "scrambled puzzle pieces water cycle",
              "sourceSite": "Openverse",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC BY 4.0",
              "attributionText": "Educational Puzzles",
              "altText": "Scrambled puzzle pieces representing steps.",
              "placementHint": "Center of slide"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": {
            "title": "",
            "type": "",
            "instructions": [],
            "svgCode": ""
          },
          "animationHints": [
            "Reveal the scrambled steps one by one."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 5
        },
        {
          "templateId": "kamalaniketan-session-12",
          "templateSlideKey": "quick_assessment",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Quick Check: Do You Understand?",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 10,
          "slideType": "hook | concept | comparison | process | summary",
          "slideTitle": "Check Your Understanding",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-Quiz"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Formative Assessment"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Verify student comprehension of key concepts.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Students can answer questions about the water cycle stages.",
          "layout": "Question & Answer",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "- What turns liquid to gas?",
            "- Where do clouds come from?",
            "- Why does rain fall?"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Quick Check:",
            "1. Which process turns water into vapor? (Evaporation)",
            "2. How are clouds formed? (Condensation/Cooling)",
            "3. What pulls water back to Earth? (Gravity/Precipitation)"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Ask the class these three questions.",
            "Use 'Show of hands' or 'Whiteboard response'.",
            "Review answers: 1) Evaporation, 2) Cooling vapor turns to liquid drops, 3) Gravity pulls it down."
          ],
          "visualPlan": "Simple question marks turning into checkmarks as questions are answered.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual feedback for assessment.",
              "searchQuery": "question mark checkmark icon",
              "sourceSite": "Openverse",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC BY 4.0",
              "attributionText": "Icon Set",
              "altText": "Question and check icons.",
              "placementHint": "Next to questions"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": {
            "title": "",
            "type": "",
            "instructions": [],
            "svgCode": ""
          },
          "animationHints": [
            "Reveal answers after students guess."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 3
        },
        {
          "templateId": "kamalaniketan-session-12",
          "templateSlideKey": "summary",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Lesson Summary: The Water Cycle",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 11,
          "slideType": "hook | concept | comparison | process | summary",
          "slideTitle": "Recap: Key Takeaways",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-Summary"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Summary of Concepts"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Reinforce the main points and vocabulary.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Students remember the three key stages and their order.",
          "layout": "Bullet Summary",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "- Evaporation: Liquid to Gas (Sun)",
            "- Condensation: Gas to Liquid (Clouds)",
            "- Precipitation: Water falls back down."
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Summary:",
            "1. **Evaporation**: Sun heats water -> Vapor rises.",
            "2. **Condensation**: Vapor cools -> Clouds form.",
            "3. **Precipitation**: Heavy clouds -> Rain/Snow falls."
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Briefly recap the three steps in order.",
            "Remind students that this cycle never stops; it is continuous.",
            "Highlight the vocabulary words one last time."
          ],
          "visualPlan": "A simplified circular diagram with just the three keywords and arrows connecting them.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual summary of the cycle.",
              "searchQuery": "simple water cycle icons",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "Public Domain",
              "attributionText": "Science Icons",
              "altText": "Simple diagram with Evaporation, Condensation, Precipitation.",
              "placementHint": "Center"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": {
            "title": "",
            "type": "",
            "instructions": [],
            "svgCode": ""
          },
          "animationHints": [
            "Fade in each step as you summarize."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 3
        },
        {
          "templateId": "kamalaniketan-session-12",
          "templateSlideKey": "homework_next_session",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Homework & Next Steps",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 12,
          "slideType": "hook | concept | comparison | process | summary",
          "slideTitle": "Your Turn: Homework & Preview",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-Homework"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Assignment & Future Topics"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Assign homework and preview the next lesson.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Students know what to do for homework and what's coming next.",
          "layout": "Two Columns",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "- Homework: Draw your own cycle.",
            "- Reflection: Where does water go after rain?",
            "- Next Session: Groundwater & Oceans."
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Homework:",
            "- Draw the Water Cycle and label all 3 steps.",
            "- Write one sentence about where puddles go.",
            "Next Time:",
            "- We will explore what happens underground!",
            "- Groundwater & Oceans."
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Assign the drawing task as homework.",
            "Ask them to think critically: 'If it rains on a roof, where does that water go?'",
            "Preview next session: We will look at what happens after precipitation (collection in oceans/lakes and groundwater)."
          ],
          "visualPlan": "Icon of a notebook for homework and an arrow pointing forward for the next lesson.",
          "assets": [
            {
